adv_u01.text: Unidad 1 - Presente Continuo y Presente Simple (1): Verbos estáticos
adv_u01.note: Pienso versus estoy pensando - dos significados
adv_u01.exp:
Unit 1 (Advanced) — Present Continuous y Present Simple con stative verbs: doble interpretación.

Los verbos de estado suelen ir en Simple, pero muchos admiten Continuous con cambio de sentido. Think: I think you're right (opinión) ↔ I'm thinking about your offer (estoy reflexionando). See: I see what you mean (entiendo) ↔ I'm seeing a specialist (me veo con/consulto). Have: I have three brothers (poseo) ↔ I'm having lunch (proceso). Taste/smell: The soup tastes salty (cualidad) ↔ She's tasting the soup (la está probando). Feel: I feel it's unfair (creo) ↔ I'm feeling dizzy (sensación, también Simple posible).

Registro: el Continuous con stative da dinamismo, a menudo tono coloquial. Pragmática: I'm loving it — eslogan publicitario, enfático, no neutro. Escritura formal suele mantenerse en Simple para hechos y opiniones. Este nivel distingue C1 de B2: cambiar la forma conscientemente por un matiz.
adv_u01.exc:
Los verbos de estado (know, believe, belong, own, contain) no suelen usarse en Continuous.

Pero think, have, see, feel, taste cambian de sentido: I'm thinking (reflexiono) vs. I think (opino).
mistakes: I am knowing him → I know him — know es de estado.
I am having a car → I have a car — posesión — simple.
This is tasting good → This tastes good — percepción — simple.

adv_u02.text: Unidad 2 – Presente continuo y presente simple (2)
adv_u02.note: Situaciones temporales versus permanentes: casos avanzados
adv_u02.exp:
Unit 2 (Advanced) — temporal vs permanente y matices emocionales del Continuous.

El Present Continuous no solo describe «ahora»:
  • Periodo temporal: I'm living with my parents while my flat is being renovated — vivo temporalmente con mis padres (permanente — I live with my parents).
  • Matiz emocional con always, constantly, forever: She's always losing her keys — siempre pierde las llaves (irritación, no neutral como Simple).
  • Tendencias en desarrollo: The climate is getting warmer — el clima se está calentando.
  • Narrativa viva en presente (contar anécdotas): So he walks in and he's waving his arms…
El Simple — para estados permanentes, verdades científicas, horarios, rasgos generales: Water boils at 100°C; The Earth orbits the Sun.

Pragmática: la elección del tiempo es una herramienta estilística. Continuous con always aporta expresividad; en texto académico no es apropiado. Alternar entre Simple descriptivo y Continuous implicado marca dominio avanzado.
adv_u02.exc:
always + Continuous = hábito irritante: She's always complaining.

Continuous subraya lo temporal: I'm living in Paris (temporal) vs. I live in Paris (permanente).
mistakes: The Earth is going round the sun → The Earth goes round the sun — hecho científico — simple.
He always is complaining → He is always complaining — orden: to be + always + -ing.
I'm usually working late → I usually work late — hábito — simple.

adv_u03.text: Unidad 3 – Pasado simple y presente perfecto
adv_u03.exp:
Unit 3 (Advanced) — Past Simple vs Present Perfect: contexto, registro, uso.

La regla básica «pasado concreto → Simple, conexión con el presente → Perfect» se complica con factores contextuales. Estructura del discurso: al dar una noticia, Perfect: A man has been arrested in connection with the robbery. Los detalles siguen en Simple: He was arrested at his home this morning. Este par «titular — desarrollo» es típico de noticias e informes.

Variación regional: el AmE usa Past Simple con just, already, yet más libremente: Did you eat yet? (AmE) vs Have you eaten yet? (BrE). En BrE académico Perfect es obligatorio con esos adverbios.

Cambio de perspectiva: It's the first/second/only time + Present Perfect: It's the first time I've seen snow. Señales: ever, never, so far, up to now, recently, lately, since → Perfect; ago, in 1999, yesterday, last week, when → Simple.

Ejemplo: I've lived in Rome for ten years (hasta ahora) ↔ I lived in Rome for ten years (periodo cerrado). En narración predomina Simple; Perfect rompe la narrativa trayendo al presente.
adv_u03.exc: El AmE usa más Past Simple donde el BrE prefiere Present Perfect: Did you eat yet? (AmE) vs. Have you eaten yet? (BrE).
mistakes: I have seen him yesterday → I saw him yesterday — pasado concreto — Past Simple.
When have you arrived? → When did you arrive? — when → Past Simple.
I live here since 2010 → I have lived here since 2010 — since → Perfect.

adv_u04.text: Unidad 4 - Pasado continuo y pasado simple
adv_u04.exp:
Unit 4 (Advanced) — Past Continuous y Past Simple en narrativa compleja.

El Past Continuous crea el fondo, el Past Simple son eventos-puntos: The sun was setting and birds were singing as we walked home — el sol se ponía, los pájaros cantaban mientras íbamos a casa. Interrupción: I was reading when the phone rang. Acciones paralelas prolongadas: While I was cooking, she was setting the table.

Funciones estilísticas:
  • Suavizado/diplomacia: I was wondering if you could help me — suena más cortés que I wonder.
  • Intención no realizada: I was going to call you, but I forgot (iba a, pero).
  • Acciones repetidas molestas: He was always turning up late — siempre llegaba tarde.

Limitaciones: los stative-verbs (know, believe, understand, belong) no suelen ir en Continuous, ni siquiera como fondo: I knew him then (no was knowing).

La elección entre Simple y Continuous en pasado define la «cámara del narrador»: punto de evento o duración del proceso.
adv_u04.exc: Los stative verbs suelen ir en Past Simple, aunque sean «fondo»: I knew him then (no was knowing).
mistakes: When I came in, she read a book (background) → she was reading a book — acción de fondo — Continuous.
I was knowing the answer → I knew the answer — stative — simple.
While I cooked, the phone rang → While I was cooking, the phone rang.

adv_u05.text: Unidad 5 - Pasado perfecto y pasado simple
adv_u05.exp:
Unit 5 (Advanced) — Past Perfect como marcador de anterioridad.

Fórmula: had + V3. Propósito — mostrar que un evento pasado precedió a otro: When I arrived, the meeting had already started — cuando llegué, la reunión ya había empezado.

Cuándo es obligatorio: con by the time: By the time we reached the theatre, the play had ended. En estilo indirecto al cambiar Past Simple/Present Perfect: She said she had seen him. Cuando se invierte el orden (consecuencia antes de causa en el texto): He was exhausted. He had run ten miles.

Cuándo no hace falta: si after/before/when ya marcan el orden: After he left, I called a taxi — basta Past Simple.

Registro literario: el Past Perfect abunda en narrativa de ficción: She had always wanted to travel. En lengua oral los nativos suelen prescindir si el orden es claro por contexto.

Ejemplo: I didn't recognise her — she had changed so much — no la reconocí: había cambiado tanto (el cambio ocurrió antes).
adv_u05.exc:
Past Perfect no hace falta si el orden es evidente por after, before, when: After he left, I called.

Con by the time Past Perfect es obligatorio: By the time we arrived, they had gone.
mistakes: When I arrived, he left (= before me) → When I arrived, he had left — antes — Past Perfect.
I had ate already → I had eaten already — 3ª forma.
By the time she came, I finished → I had finished.

adv_u06.text: Unidad 6 – Presente perfecto continuo y presente perfecto
adv_u06.note: Énfasis en el proceso vs. en el resultado
adv_u06.exp:
Unit 6 (Advanced) — Present Perfect Continuous vs Present Perfect: proceso vs resultado.

PPC (have been + -ing) enfoca duración, proceso, actividad reciente: I've been painting the kitchen — he estado pintando la cocina (quizá no acabé; importa el proceso/sus huellas). PP (have + V3) enfoca resultado, cantidad, completitud: I've painted three rooms — he pintado tres habitaciones (resultado).

Ambas posibles con verbos de duración (live, work, study, teach, wait) describiendo estado actual: I've lived / I've been living here for ten years. Con verbos de estado (stative) — solo Perfect: I've known her for years (no PPC).

Pragmática: I've been trying to reach you — con tono de disculpa/explicación. Have you been crying? — se ven las huellas. Someone has been using my computer — señales de intervención.

Señales: for, since, all day, recently, lately — con ambas; how many, twice, three times — solo Perfect (cuenta resultados). How long suele tirar al PPC para subrayar el proceso.
adv_u06.exc:
Los stative verbs no van en PPC: I've known him for years (no been knowing).

PPC enfatiza el proceso, Perfect el resultado: I've been painting (sigue) vs. I've painted the room (terminado).
mistakes: I've been knowing her → I've known her — stative.
I've read three books this week (process focus) — para cantidad — Perfect; para proceso — I've been reading.
She has been finished → She has finished.

adv_u07.text: Unidad 7 - Pasado perfecto continuo, pasado perfecto y pasado continuo
adv_u07.note: Tres tiempos pasados en una narrativa
adv_u07.exp:
Unit 7 (Advanced) — tres niveles pasados: Past Continuous, Past Perfect, Past Perfect Continuous en narrativa coherente.

Past Continuous — escena/fondo; Past Perfect — evento anterior terminado; Past Perfect Continuous (had been + -ing) — acción prolongada anterior con vínculo causal.

Patrón narrativo típico: She looked exhausted. She had been working since 6 a.m. and hadn't eaten all day when the call finally came. — Aquí PPC explica su estado, Past Perfect fija «no había comido», Past Simple marca el evento-punto.

PPC vs Past Perfect: When I arrived, they had had dinner (hecho: cena terminada) ↔ When I arrived, they had been having dinner (duraba hasta ese momento, quizá no acabada). The roads were wet because it had rained (hecho) ↔ because it had been raining (duración enfatizada).

Limitación: los stative-verbs no forman PPC: I had known him, no had been knowing. Registro: el PPC es común en prosa literaria y biográfica, raro en academia.
adv_u07.exc:
Past Perfect Continuous no se usa con stative verbs.

Past Perfect Continuous subraya duración hasta un punto del pasado: She had been waiting for an hour when he arrived.
mistakes: She had been knowing him → She had known him.
He was tired because he ran → he had been running — causa anterior — PPC.
When I came, she had been leaving (action done) → she had left.

adv_u08.text: Unidad 8 - Tiempo presente y pasado: descripción general
adv_u08.note: Revisión sistemática del presente y del pasado.
adv_u08.exp:
Unit 8 (Advanced) — repaso de tiempos presentes y pasados: elección sistemática.

La decisión se toma según cuatro ejes:
  1. Momento/periodo: punto → Simple; duración → Continuous.
  2. Completitud: acabado con actualidad → Perfect; desligado del presente → Past Simple.
  3. Proceso vs resultado: Continuous vs Perfect.
  4. Conexión con el presente: sí → Perfect; no → Past.
Estos ejes se cruzan: Present Perfect Continuous = periodo + proceso + conexión.

Desviaciones de registro:
  • Titulares de prensa suelen reemplazar Perfect por Simple: Plane crashes in storm.
  • El estilo jurídico-oficial prefiere Perfect: The defendant has admitted…
  • En narrativa literaria domina el pasado, Present Perfect aparece en diálogos/monólogo interno.

Errores típicos: since/for exigen Perfect; ago/fecha concreta exigen Past Simple; preguntas when sobre momento exacto — Past Simple (When did you arrive?).

Regla resumen: Perfect — «puente» entre pasado y presente; Past Simple — pasado «cerrado»; Continuous en cualquier zona — «la cámara acerca el proceso».
adv_u08.exc: En titulares de prensa suele aparecer Past Simple en vez de Present Perfect: Plane crashes in storm.
mistakes: I am here since morning → I have been here since morning — since → Perfect.
She has left an hour ago → She left an hour ago — ago → Past Simple.
Did you ever be there? → Have you ever been there?

adv_u09.note: Las sutilezas de elegir entre voluntad y destino.
adv_u09.exp:
Unit 9 (Advanced) — will vs be going to: más allá de la regla básica.

El contraste básico «espontáneo vs planificado» es solo un eje. En C1 importan:
  • Tipo de evidencia para predecir: be going to se apoya en signos visibles (Look at those clouds — it's going to rain); will en conocimiento/experiencia/deducción (It will probably rain — it usually does in April).
  • Planes: be going to — intención tomada antes del momento de hablar; will — decisión tomada ahora: — The phone's ringing. — I'll get it.
  • Registro: will es más neutro en formal/escrito; be going to coloquial, especialmente AmE.
  • Promesas, ofrecimientos, amenazas, negativas: solo will/won't: I'll help you. I won't tell anyone.

Matices: was/were going to suele indicar intención no realizada: I was going to call, but I forgot. There will be para pronóstico (There will be rain tomorrow), pero there is going to be para evento esperado (There's going to be trouble).
adv_u09.exc:
will para decisiones espontáneas y predicciones; be going to para planes y signos visibles.

Ambos permiten predecir, pero going to con apoyo en hechos actuales: Look at those clouds — it's going to rain.
mistakes: I will meet her tomorrow (plan) → I'm going to meet her / I'm meeting her — plan.
The phone rings — I'm going to get it → I'll get it — espontáneo.
Look, it will rain → it's going to rain — signos.

adv_u10.text: Unidad 10 – Presente simple y continuo para el futuro
adv_u10.exp:
Unit 10 (Advanced) — tiempos presentes para el futuro.

Present Simple para futuro — solo para horarios, programas, fechas inamovibles, fuera del control del hablante: The flight leaves at 7.45. The conference begins on Monday. The film starts at 8. También en subordinadas de tiempo/condición: When he arrives, I'll tell him; If it rains, we'll stay home.

Present Continuous para futuro — para arreglos personales con detalle concreto (llamada, reserva, invitación): I'm meeting Ann at 6. We're having dinner with the Smiths on Friday. Requiere acuerdo/planificación.

Matices de fijeza: The train leaves at 5 (horario — casi ley) > I'm leaving at 5 (intención firme/acuerdo) > I'm going to leave at 5 (intención, menos formalizada) > I'll leave at 5 (decisión/promesa ahora).

Limitación: Present Continuous futuro no sirve para pronósticos de clima/naturaleza: NO It's raining tomorrow, sino It's going to rain / It will rain. Para horarios de transporte Continuous es incorrecto: The train leaves at 6, no is leaving.
adv_u10.exc:
Present Simple futuro — solo para horarios: The train leaves at 6.

Present Continuous — para arreglos personales: I'm meeting Tom at 5.
mistakes: I meet Tom tomorrow (arranged) → I'm meeting Tom tomorrow — arreglo — Continuous.
The film is starting at 8 (schedule) → The film starts at 8 — horario — Simple.
When will he comes? → When does he come? / When is he coming?

adv_u100.note: Así fue / Tan genial fue / Apenas lo había hecho...
adv_u100.exp:
Unit 100 — inversión (lección 2): so/such, hardly, no sooner.

Fenómeno: segundo conjunto de construcciones invertidas: muy formal, con tinte literario.

Modelos:
  • So + adj/adv + be/aux + sujeto + cláusula con that: So great was his anger that he left. — Tal era su ira que se marchó.
  • Such + be + sujeto (+ that): Such was the force of the explosion that windows shattered.
  • Hardly/Scarcely + had + sujeto + V3 + when…: Hardly had I arrived when the phone rang. — Apenas había llegado cuando sonó el teléfono.
  • No sooner + had + sujeto + V3 + than…: No sooner had she spoken than he replied.
  • Inversión en las condicionales: Had I known… Were I you… Should you need… (véase Unit 85).
Pares críticos: Hardly/Scarcely … when (no than); No sooner … than (no when).

Registro: narrativa literaria, documentos oficiales, discurso.

Contraste: equivalente neutro: As soon as I arrived, the phone rang. La versión invertida dramatiza la sucesión de los hechos.
mistakes: No sooner he had left when it rained. → No sooner had he left than it rained.
Hardly I had arrived than → Hardly had I arrived when…
Such was a his anger → Such was his anger that… — sin artículo.

adv_u11.text: Unidad 11 - Futuro continuo y futuro perfecto (continuo)
adv_u11.note: estaré trabajando / habré terminado / habré estado trabajando
adv_u11.exp:
Unit 11 (Advanced) — Future Continuous, Future Perfect y Future Perfect Continuous.

Future Continuous (will be + -ing): proceso en un punto del futuro (This time tomorrow I'll be flying to Tokyo); algo planeado/esperado en el curso normal (I'll be seeing her later — «la veré» como parte natural); pregunta cortés sin presión (Will you be using the car tomorrow? — menos invasiva que Do you need the car?).

Future Perfect (will have + V3): acción acabada para un momento del futuro. Marcadores: by + time, by the time + clause. By 2030 I'll have graduated. By the time you arrive, we'll have finished dinner.

Future Perfect Continuous (will have been + -ing): proceso que dura hasta un momento futuro. In June, I'll have been working here for ten years. Se subraya la duración.

Registro y límites: Future Perfect Continuous — forma relativamente rara, habitual en contextos biográficos y de aniversario. Los stative no admiten Continuous: I will have known him for 20 years, no will have been knowing. Future Continuous reduce presión en peticiones — paso pragmático importante en correspondencia empresarial.
adv_u11.exc:
Future Perfect — normalmente con by + tiempo: By 2030 I'll have graduated.

Future Continuous puede significar «acción planeada/esperada en el futuro»: I'll be seeing him tomorrow.
mistakes: At 5 tomorrow I will work → I will be working — proceso en un momento concreto.
By next year I will finish → I will have finished — para el momento — Perfect.
I will have been knowing → I will have known — stative.

adv_u12.exp:
Unit 12 (Advanced) — construcción be to + infinitive.

Fórmula: am/is/are/was/were + to + V. Sobre todo registro formal y escrito (prensa, anuncios oficiales, diplomacia, instrucciones).

Valores:
  • Plan/acuerdo oficial: The Prime Minister is to visit Japan next week — el primer ministro visitará Japón la próxima semana.
  • Orden, instrucción: You are to report to security by 9 a.m. — debe presentarse a seguridad a las 9.
  • Prohibición: No one is to enter the room — nadie debe entrar en la sala.
  • Condición (en if-clauses): If we are to succeed, we must act now — si queremos triunfar, hay que actuar.
  • Destino (literario): He was to become a great artist — estaba destinado a ser un gran artista.

Pasado: was/were to + V puede indicar intención cumplida o no. Para «debía haber hecho, pero no hice» se usa was/were to have + V3: He was to have arrived yesterday (debía llegar, pero no llegó).

En lengua oral esta forma apenas aparece — su nicho es el estilo oficial.
adv_u12.exc:
be to + inf se usa en noticias y contextos oficiales: The President is to visit Japan.

También en condicionales: If we are to succeed, we must....
mistakes: I am to meet my friend (personal plan) → I'm meeting my friend — be to no para planes personales.
The PM is visit France → The PM is to visit France — to obligatorio.
He was to died young → He was to die young — infinitivo sin -ed.

adv_u13.text: Unidad 13 - Otras formas de expresar el futuro
adv_u13.note: estar a punto de / estar debido a / estar obligado a
adv_u13.exp:
Unit 13 (Advanced) — medios adicionales para expresar futuro.

Be about to + inf — «a punto de»: The train is about to leave — el tren está a punto de salir. A menudo con just: I was just about to call you. Futuro muy próximo, sin referencia calendaria.

Be due to + inf / be due + time — «según lo previsto»: The flight is due to arrive at 18.00; The book is due out in May. Registro neutro-oficial.

Be bound to + inf — «seguro, inevitable»: She's bound to win — she's the best. Predicción firme.

Be likely/unlikely to + inf — probabilidad: It's likely to rain tonight. Alternativa: It's likely that it will rain.

Be on the point/verge/brink of + -ing — al borde de: The company is on the verge of bankruptcy.

Be set to + inf — preparado/debe (estilo prensa): Prices are set to rise.

Enriquecen el repertorio y se distinguen por grado de certeza y proximidad temporal: be about to (inmediato) → be due to (según calendario) → be likely to (probable) → be bound to (casi seguro).
adv_u13.exc: be about to = a punto de; be due to = según horario; be bound to = ocurrirá seguro; be on the verge/point of + -ing.
mistakes: I'm about to leaving → I'm about to leave — infinitivo.
The train is due arrive → The train is due to arrive — to obligatorio.
He is bound fail → He is bound to fail.

adv_u14.note: Ella dijo que vendría. Iba a dimitir.
adv_u14.exp:
Unit 14 (Advanced) — Future in the Past: mirada desde el pasado al futuro.

Cuando la narrativa va en pasado pero hay que señalar algo futuro respecto a ese punto, se usan formas especiales.

Would + V — «habría/resultaría/ocurriría luego»: In 1920 she published her first novel. It would become a bestseller.

Was/were going to + V — intención en el pasado, a menudo no cumplida: He was going to apologise, but he changed his mind.

Was/were to + V — destino, predeterminado: He was to become president ten years later. La forma perfecta was to have + V3 subraya no cumplimiento: The meeting was to have taken place on Monday, but was cancelled.

Past Continuous — para eventos planeados: He was leaving the next day.

Was about to + V — a punto de: She was about to speak when the door opened.

El uso principal — estilo indirecto (He said he would come), narrativas biográficas y ficción. Future in the Past distingue a un narrador maduro capaz de proyectar el futuro dentro del pasado.
adv_u14.exc:
Future in the Past: would, was/were going to, Past Continuous, was/were to.

was going to suele indicar que se planeó pero no se hizo: I was going to call, but forgot.
mistakes: He said he will come → He said he would come — desplazamiento.
I was going call → I was going to call — to.
She was to died → She was to die.

adv_u15.exp:
Unit 15 (Advanced) — capacidad y permiso: can, could, be able to, be allowed to, manage to.

Capacidad en pasado: could — capacidad general (She could read at the age of four). Para una acción concreta exitosa se prefiere was/were able to, managed to, succeeded in -ing: The door was locked, but I managed to open it (NO could open). Excepción — en negativas y con verbos de percepción (see, hear, smell, understand, remember): I couldn't see him in the dark — perfectamente correcto.

Permiso: can — coloquial-neutro; may — formal-cortés; be allowed to — subraya regla externa (You're not allowed to smoke here).

Epistémico vs deóntico: can expresa a la vez capacidad («puedo»), permiso («se puede»), posibilidad («a veces»: It can be cold here in winter). Could también expresa probabilidad suave (That could be true).

Registro: be able to es insustituible donde los modales no funcionan: tras otro modal (I'd like to be able to swim), en infinitivo/gerundio (being able to drive), en perfecto (I've been able to solve it).
adv_u15.exc:
could — capacidad general en pasado; para una acción exitosa concreta — was/were able to / managed to: I was able to escape (no could escape).

Pero en negativas/con percepción — couldn't ok: I couldn't see him.
mistakes: Yesterday I could win the match → I was able to win / managed to win — evento concreto.
He is allowed entering → He is allowed to enter — to.
I can to swim → I can swim — sin to.

adv_u16.exp:
Unit 16 (Advanced) — will, would, used to para hábitos y patrones típicos.

Will (presente) — conducta o propiedad característica/típica: Oil will float on water. He'll sit for hours, staring out of the window. Con énfasis en will puede expresar irritación: He WILL keep interrupting me!

Would (pasado) — acciones repetidas en el pasado, episodios típicos narrativos: Every summer we would go to the seaside. She would always bring me flowers. Limitación clave: would no va con valores de estado: no se dice *I would have a red bike (estado); para eso — used to.

Used to + inf — acciones y estados en el pasado ya no ciertos: I used to live in Oxford. She used to have long hair. El contraste con el presente es parte del significado.

Registro: el would narrativo es típico en prosa literaria y memorialística, da ritmo de recuerdos. Used to es neutral. Would always/forever + V a menudo nostálgico o con ligera irritación.
adv_u16.exc: used to — solo pasado; would — acciones repetidas en pasado, no estados: would visit ok, pero would have a car.
mistakes: I would live in Paris (state) → I used to live in Paris — estados — used to.
He use to smoke → He used to smoke.
Did he used to? → Did he use to? — tras did — use.

adv_u17.exp:
Unit 17 (Advanced) — may y might: probabilidad y permiso.

Valor epistémico (probabilidad): escala graduada de certeza: must (seguro) > will (muy probable) > should (esperado) > may (posible, ~50%) > might/could (quizá, menor). She may be in her office — quizá esté en su despacho. He might come later — puede venir luego.

Pasado: may/might have + V3 — «quizá hizo»: She may have left already — quizá ya se fue. Might have done también expresa reproche/posibilidad no realizada: You might have told me! — ¡podrías haberme dicho!

Valor deóntico (permiso): May I come in? — formal-cortés. May en normas: Students may use the library until 10. Might en este sentido es raro y arcaico.

Diferencias may vs might: might suena menos seguro y más hipotético; en condicionales might es casi obligado: If you asked him, he might help. En afirmación neutra son intercambiables. May not = quizá no; might not — análogo, matiz de menor probabilidad.
adv_u17.exc:
may/might have + V3 = quizá hizo en pasado.

might suele sonar menos seguro que may.

En peticiones formales may I es más cortés que can I.
mistakes: He may to come → He may come — sin to.
She might is right → She might be right — infinitivo.
He may came → He may have come — para el pasado.

adv_u18.note: Coerción interna versus externa en C1
adv_u18.exp:
Unit 18 (Advanced) — must y have (got) to: obligación y deducción.

Deóntico (obligación): must — subjetivo, interno o el hablante como autoridad: I must call my mother (decisión propia); Students must wear uniforms (regla de la institución/hablante). Have to / have got to — obligación externa, regla externa: I have to work on Saturdays (lo exige el jefe). En AmE have to desplaza a must casi en todo.

Pasado: must no tiene pasado deóntico — se usa had to: I had to stay late yesterday.

Epistémico (deducción): must — suposición firme: He must be tired — he's been working all day. Pasado: must have + V3: She must have forgotten — seguramente olvidó.

Negación — trampa clave:
  • mustn't = prohibición («no se debe»): You mustn't smoke here.
  • don't have to = ausencia de necesidad («no hace falta»): You don't have to come.
  • can't — negación epistémica de must: He can't be serious (= no puede ser).

Registro: must más formal, escrito; have to neutro-coloquial; have got to coloquial.
adv_u18.exc:
must — convicción interna; have to — obligación externa.

Negación: mustn't = prohibición; don't have to = no hace falta.
mistakes: You mustn't come (= no need) → You don't have to come — no hace falta.
I must to go → I must go.
I must called her yesterday → I had to call — obligación en pasado — had to.

adv_u19.text: Unidad 19 - No es necesario, no es necesario y no es necesario
adv_u19.exp:
Unit 19 (Advanced) — tres formas de «no es necesario»: needn't, don't need to, don't have to.

Needn't + V (modal, sin to) — falta de necesidad desde la perspectiva del hablante: You needn't wait — I'll catch up. Más en BrE, formal.

Don't need to + V (léxico) — ausencia de necesidad como hecho: You don't need to book in advance. Neutro.

Don't have to + V — ausencia de obligación: You don't have to answer if you don't want to. El más frecuente en conversación.

Diferencia clave en pasado:
  • Needn't have + V3 = hizo en vano: You needn't have cooked — we've eaten (cocinó, pero fue innecesario).
  • Didn't need to + V = no hizo falta (normalmente no se hizo): I didn't need to go to the bank (y no fui).
Muchas veces el contexto decide si la acción se realizó.

Pragmática: needn't have lleva matiz «lástima que lo hicieras, era innecesario»; es un punto sutil, importante en ensayos y diálogos.
adv_u19.exc: needn't + V = no hay obligación; needn't have + V3 = hizo en vano; didn't need to + V = no hizo falta (y normalmente no se hizo).
mistakes: You needn't to go → You needn't go — needn't sin to.
I needn't went → I needn't have gone — pasado — have V3.
She doesn't need go → She doesn't need to go — don't need + to.

adv_u20.text: Unidad 20 – Debería, debería y hubiera sido mejor
adv_u20.exp:
Unit 20 (Advanced) — should, ought to, had better: consejos y recomendaciones.

Should / ought to — casi sinónimos, obligación moral o racional leve: You should/ought to see a doctor. Ought to algo más formal y refiere a una norma objetiva; should más versátil. Negación: shouldn't / ought not to. Las preguntas con ought to son raras — casi siempre should.

Had better (+ V, sin to) — consejo apremiante en situación concreta, a menudo con advertencia sobre consecuencias: You'd better leave now, or you'll miss the train. Negación: had better not: You'd better not be late. NO es norma general, sino recomendación ad hoc.

Pasado (reproche):
  • should/ought to have + V3 = debería haber hecho, pero no lo hizo: You should have told me.
  • shouldn't/ought not to have + V3 = no debería haber hecho, pero lo hizo: I shouldn't have eaten so much.

Registro: ought to suena algo arcaico en AmE; en BrE vivo. Had better — coloquial, con insistencia, no apropiado para consejo neutral (para eso You might want to / It might be a good idea to).
adv_u20.exc:
had better — consejo apremiante, a menudo con amenaza; should — consejo neutro.

ought to = should (algo más formal); negación: ought not to.
mistakes: You had better to go → You had better go — sin to.
I would better go → I had better go — had, no would.
You should to call → You should call.

adv_u21.text: Unidad 21 - Verbos de enlace: ser, aparecer, parecer, convertirse, obtener
adv_u21.note: Ella parece cansada. No se enojó. Se hizo oscuro.
adv_u21.exp:
Unit 21 (Advanced) — verbos copulativos (linking / copular verbs).

La cópula une el sujeto con el atributo (adjetivo o sustantivo) que describe al sujeto, no la acción. Tras la cópula va un adjetivo, no un adverbio.

Cópulas estáticas: be, seem, appear, look, feel, sound, smell, taste, remain, stay, keep: She seems happy. This cheese tastes odd. He remained silent.

Cópulas de cambio de estado:
  • become — universal, suele llevar sustantivo/adjetivo: He became a doctor.
  • get — coloquial, con adjetivo: It's getting dark; I got tired.
  • grow — gradual: grow old, grow tired.
  • turn — brusco/colores: The leaves turned yellow; He turned pale.
  • go — negativo/antinatural: go bad, go blind, go mad.
  • come — resultado positivo: dreams come true, wishes come true.
  • fall — súbito: fall ill, fall silent, fall asleep.

Contexto: look good (cópula, se ve bien) ↔ look carefully (acción, mirar con atención). Los mismos verbos pueden ser cópula o pleno — la diferencia es sintáctica.
adv_u21.exc:
Tras linking verbs — adjetivo, no adverbio: She looks tired, no tiredly.

get suele = volverse: It's getting dark.
mistakes: She seems tiredly → She seems tired — adjetivo.
He became to angry → He became angry — sin to.
The milk turned to sour → The milk turned sour.

adv_u22.note: Voz pasiva: funciones y selecciones en el C1
adv_u22.exp:
Unit 22 (Advanced) — voz pasiva: funciones y selección.

Fórmula: be + V3 en todos los tiempos. Tres motivos discursivos para elegir pasiva:
  1. Agente desconocido/irrelevante: My bike has been stolen.
  2. Agente obvio o genérico: She was arrested yesterday (por la policía, claro).
  3. Foco informativo: el tema es el objeto de la acción: The Sistine Chapel was painted by Michelangelo.

Registro académico y oficial usa la pasiva por impersonalidad: The experiment was conducted under controlled conditions.

Get-passive — variante coloquial, con matiz de sorpresa o negatividad: She got fired last week; We got caught in the rain. Se asocia a eventos «accidentales/emocionales».

Limitaciones: los intransitivos no forman pasiva (happen, arrive, die, come, go, fall): no se dice *It was happened. Los transitivos de «estado de posesión» (have, lack, possess, fit, suit, resemble) tampoco suelen pasivarse.

By vs with: by — agente (written by Dickens); with — instrumento (cut with a knife); filled with (lleno de) — caso especial.
adv_u22.exc:
No todos los verbos admiten pasiva (p. ej. have = poseer, fit, suit).

by — agente; with — instrumento: cut by him with a knife.
mistakes: The book was wrote → was written — V3.
It was happened → It happened — happen no admite pasiva.
He was died → He died — die no admite pasiva.

adv_u23.note: Estando hecho / por hacer / habiéndose hecho
adv_u23.exp:
Unit 23 (Advanced) — formas no personales pasivas.

Infinitivo pasivo — (to) be + V3: The report needs to be finished by Monday. This problem has to be solved. Típico tras modales y verbos como need, want, have.

Gerundio pasivo — being + V3: She hates being criticised. He remembers being praised as a child. Se usa cuando el sujeto implícito es el objeto de la acción.

Formas perfectas pasivas:
  • Infinitivo perfecto pasivo: to have been + V3: He claims to have been robbed.
  • Gerundio perfecto pasivo: having been + V3: Having been warned, she was careful.

Participio pasivo (Past Participle) como subordinada reducida: The letter written yesterday is on the desk (= which was written); Being followed, she walked faster (= because she was being followed).

Registro: las formas -ing e infinitivas pasivas son típicas de texto formal/académico. El estilo coloquial suele sustituirlas por activo o subordinada: After I was told → Having been told / After being told. Dominarlas es signo de C1+.
adv_u23.exc: Gerundio pasivo: being done. Infinitivo pasivo: to be done. Gerundio perfecto pasivo: having been done.
mistakes: I hate be criticized → I hate being criticized — -ing.
This needs to done → This needs to be done.
After been told → After being told / Having been told.

adv_u24.text: Unidad 24 - Diferentes formas de usar Pasivo
adv_u24.note: Pasivo con dos objetos y otros casos avanzados
adv_u24.exp:
Unit 24 (Advanced) — construcciones pasivas no estándar.

Pasiva con dos objetos. Verbos como give, send, offer, tell, show, teach, pay, lend, promise tienen dos objetos → dos pasivas posibles: She gave me a present → I was given a present (by her) / A present was given to me. La forma con persona en sujeto suele preferirse en habla — es más temática.

Verbos excepción: explain, describe, suggest, mention, announce, say no admiten sujeto-persona en pasiva: The rule was explained to me, NO *I was explained the rule.

Pasiva impersonal con it: It is said/believed/thought that… — típico de noticias y academia: It was decided that the meeting would be postponed. Alternativa — personal passive (véase Unit 25).

Get-passive: coloquial, a menudo con connotación negativa: He got sacked; We got soaked.

Pseudo-pasivo / pasivo descriptivo: distinguir estado de acción: The door was open (estado) ↔ The door was opened by Tom (acción). Adjetivo tras be no es pasiva aunque lo parezca.

Causativo have/get: I had/got my hair cut — «me cortaron el pelo», idea pasiva con forma activa.
adv_u24.exc: Verbos con dos objetos pueden tener dos pasivas: She was given a book / A book was given to her. Se prefiere la primera (persona-sujeto).
mistakes: A book was given her → A book was given to her — to necesario.
I was explained the rule → The rule was explained to me — explain no admite person-subject.
He was said that… → He was told that… / It was said that… — say ≠ tell.

adv_u25.text: Unidad 25 – Informar con Pasiva: Se dice que…
adv_u25.exp:
Unit 25 (Advanced) — reporting con pasiva: It is said that… / He is said to…

Para transmisión impersonal de opiniones y datos — en noticias, textos académicos, reseñas — se usan dos construcciones sinónimas:
  1. It + be + V3 + that-clause: It is said that she's the best in her field.
  2. Subject + be + V3 + to-infinitive: She is said to be the best in her field.
Ambas significan «se dice/afirma que…», sin nombrar la fuente.

Verbos de este tipo: say, believe, think, consider, know, report, claim, allege, suppose, understand, expect, find, rumour.

Tiempos del infinitivo:
  • Presente/general: He is said to live in Paris (vive ahora).
  • Pasado: to have + V3: He is said to have lived in Paris (vivió antes).
  • Duración: to be + -ing: He is said to be living in Paris (vive ahora, en curso).
  • Perfecto continuo: to have been + -ing: He is said to have been living there for ten years.

Registro y distancia: la construcción señala cautela y distancia — el autor no confirma, reformula. Insustituible en periodismo: The suspect is alleged to have entered through the window.
adv_u25.exc:
Dos estructuras: It is said that he is rich = He is said to be rich.

Para el pasado: He is said to have left.
mistakes: He is said that he is rich → He is said to be rich / It is said that he is rich.
He is said to left → He is said to have left — pasado — have V3.
It said that… → It is said that….

adv_u26.text: Unidad 26 – Preguntas Wh: quién, quién, cuál, cómo, de quién
adv_u26.note: Preguntas complejas con preposiciones y opciones quién/quién
adv_u26.exp:
Unit 26 (Advanced) — preguntas wh- complejas: who/whom, which, how, whose, what.

Who vs whom: en inglés actual domina who; whom es estrictamente formal, obligatorio solo inmediatamente tras preposición: To whom should I address the complaint? En habla la preposición va al final: Who should I address the complaint to?

Which vs what: which — elección dentro de un conjunto limitado (Which of these books do you prefer?); what — pregunta abierta sin conjunto (What do you want to read?).

Whose — posesión: Whose bag is this?

How + adj/adv para grado: How tall is he? How often do you go there? How long have you lived here?

Preguntas indirectas/embedded usan orden directo, sin do/does/did: Do you know where he lives? (NO where does he live). Regla que incluso avanzados rompen.

What…like vs How: What is she like? — ¿cómo es de carácter? How is she? — ¿cómo está (salud)? What does she look like? — ¿qué aspecto tiene? How does it work? — ¿cómo funciona?

Pragmática: whom en cartas formales y reclamaciones conserva autoridad anticuada. En correo electrónico más who.
adv_u26.exc:
whom — caso objeto formal; en registro informal — who: Who did you see?.

Preposición al final (informal) o antes de whom (formal): To whom did you speak?.
mistakes: Who did you give it? → Who(m) did you give it to? — falta preposición.
Whose is this car yours? → Is this car yours? / Whose car is this?.
How is she like? → What is she like? — carácter — what.

adv_u27.text: Unidad 27 - Preguntas negativas; preguntas de eco; preguntas con cláusulas de chat
adv_u27.note: ¿No es ella encantadora? Ella se va, ¿verdad?
adv_u27.exp:
Unit 27 (Advanced) — tipos no estándar de preguntas.

Preguntas negativas (Isn't she lovely? Didn't you know?) expresan no una consulta, sino emoción: sorpresa, irritación, confirmación. Haven't we met before? — ¿no nos hemos visto ya? Why didn't you tell me? — reproche. En entonación exclamativa: Isn't it beautiful! — ¡qué bonito!

Echo questions repiten parte de lo dicho con entonación ascendente, expresando sorpresa o repregunta: — I'm moving to Tokyo. — You're moving WHERE? En la pregunta — orden afirmativo con acento enfático.

Declarative questions — forma de afirmación pero con entonación ascendente y función de pregunta: You've finished already? Expresan sorpresa o petición de confirmación.

Tag questions: confirmación de respuesta esperada. Reglas: afirmación + tag negativo (She's Russian, isn't she?); negación + tag positivo (You don't smoke, do you?). Casos especiales: I am → aren't I?; Let's → shall we?; imperativo → will/would/can you?

Pragmática: entonación descendente en tag — «lo sé, confírmalo»; ascendente — «¿es realmente así?».
adv_u27.exc:
Las preguntas negativas expresan sorpresa/emoción: Isn't she lovely!.

Tag questions: afirm. → tag neg, neg → tag pos.
mistakes: You like it, don't you like? → …, don't you? — forma breve.
He is tired, isn't it? → …, isn't he? — pronombre del sujeto.
Let's go, don't we? → Let's go, shall we?.

adv_u28.text: Unidad 28 - Diferentes tipos de objetos después de los verbos
adv_u28.exp:
Unit 28 (Advanced) — complementos del verbo: tipos de complementación.

Cada verbo elige su estructura complementaria. Patrones básicos:
  • Verb + object + noun (object complement): They elected him president; We call him Bob.
  • Verb + object + adjective: She painted the door red; I find this book fascinating; He drove me crazy.
  • Verb + object + infinitive (con to): She asked him to help; I want you to listen.
  • Verb + object + bare infinitive (tras make, let, have + verbos de percepción): Let me go; I saw him leave.
  • Verb + object + -ing: I heard her singing; I saw them running.
  • Verb + object + past participle: I want it done by noon; She had her car repaired.

Diferencia percepción total vs parcial: I saw him cross the street (lo vi todo) vs I saw him crossing the street (lo vi durante el cruce).

Semántica del «predicativo del objeto»: elect, appoint, name, call, consider, find, declare, prove, keep, leave + object + complement forman «mini-oración» dentro de la principal: I consider him [a friend] / [honest] / [to be reliable].

Registro: consider/find sb to be + adj más formal que sin to be.
adv_u28.exc: Algunos verbos exigen object + complement: elect him president, paint it red, consider him a friend.
mistakes: They elected him as president (in formal BrE ok, but often) → They elected him president — sin as.
She painted the wall in red → She painted the wall red.
I find him as rude → I find him rude.

adv_u29.note: Ella me dio un libro. / Ella me regaló un libro.
adv_u29.exp:
Unit 29 (Advanced) — verbos con dos objetos (ditransitivos).

Los dos objetos los rigen verbos de dar, enviar, informar: give, send, offer, show, teach, tell, lend, buy, make, cook, get, pay, promise.

Dos patrones sintácticos:
  1. Verb + Indirect Object (Recipient) + Direct Object (Thing): I gave Tom the book.
  2. Verb + Direct Object + to/for + Recipient: I gave the book to Tom.

To vs for: to con give, send, tell, show, pay, offer, lend, promise, hand, pass, read; for con buy, get, make, cook, find, order, save, leave (receptor = beneficiario, no destinatario).

Estructura informativa: la elección del orden se relaciona con tema/rema: lo nuevo al final. I gave Tom the book — nuevo = libro. I gave the book to Tom — nuevo = Tom. Con pronombres suele ser obligatorio el segundo orden: Give it to me (no *give me it en AmE).

Verbos excepción — solo patrón con to: explain, describe, suggest, announce, mention, introduce, admit, confess, report. No *explain me the rule — solo explain the rule to me. Suggest exige cláusula that o -ing: She suggested that I go / She suggested going.
adv_u29.exc: explain, describe, suggest no admiten person-object: explain it to me, no explain me it.
mistakes: He explained me the rule → He explained the rule to me.
She suggested me to go → She suggested (that) I (should) go.
Give to me the book → Give me the book / Give the book to me.

adv_u30.note: Recordar/comprender/decidir: qué forma y por qué
adv_u30.exp:
Unit 30 (Advanced) — verbo + -ing o + to-infinitive: panorama.

Solo gerundio (-ing): enjoy, avoid, finish, mind, miss, practise, suggest, consider, deny, admit, delay, postpone, resist, risk, imagine, involve, keep, fancy, can't help, can't stand, give up, put off, feel like. I enjoy reading; She suggested going; We can't afford to waste time.

Solo infinitivo (to + V): want, hope, decide, plan, expect, promise, refuse, offer, manage, afford, agree, fail, seem, appear, tend, pretend, intend, threaten, choose, learn, deserve. I hope to see you soon.

Ambas formas con sentido distinto:
  • remember/forget + -ing = recuerdo del pasado; + to V = obligación futura: I remember locking the door vs Remember to lock the door.
  • stop + -ing = cesar proceso; + to V = parar para: He stopped smoking vs He stopped to smoke.
  • regret + -ing = lamentar pasado; + to inform/say/tell = informar con pesar formal: We regret to inform you…
  • go on + -ing = seguir con lo mismo; + to V = pasar a otro: He went on talking vs He went on to become a minister.

Ambas sin cambio claro: begin, start, continue, bother, love, like, hate, prefer (matices leves: -ing placer general, to V intención concreta).
mistakes: I remember to lock the door (I did it) → I remember locking it — pasado — -ing.
He stopped to smoke (= quit) → He stopped smoking — dejó.
I regret to tell him yesterday → I regret telling him.

adv_u31.note: empezar a hacer/hacer: casos sin diferencia y con diferencia
adv_u31.exp:
Unit 31 (Advanced) — gerundio vs infinitivo, construcciones especiales.

Verbos de percepción + object + bare inf / -ing:
  • see/hear/watch/feel/notice + sb + V — evento completo: I saw her cross the street (vi todo el cruce).
  • + sb + -ing — en curso: I saw her crossing the street (la vi durante el cruce).

Need/want/require/deserve + -ing con sentido pasivo: The car needs servicing = needs to be serviced; This shirt wants ironing. Formas -ing y pasiva intercambiables.

Frases idiomáticas con -ing: It's worth + -ing; It's no use / no good + -ing; There's no point (in) + -ing; Have difficulty/trouble + -ing; Spend/waste time + -ing; Be busy + -ing.

Try to V vs try -ing: try to V = intentar con esfuerzo; try -ing = probar como experimento: Try to open the door (intenta abrirla) vs Try opening it (prueba a abrirla, a ver).

Mean to V vs mean -ing: mean to = tener intención (I meant to call you); mean -ing = implicar (Being a parent means making sacrifices).

Be used to + -ing (estar acostumbrado) vs used to + V (solía) — construcciones distintas, error frecuente.
adv_u31.exc:
Sin cambio: begin/start/continue + -ing / to V.

Con cambio: try to (intentar) vs. try -ing (probar como experimento); mean to (intentar) vs. mean -ing (implicar).
mistakes: I tried to open the window (= experimented) → I tried opening the window — experimento.
It means to wait → It means waiting — implica.
I didn't mean hurting you → I didn't mean to hurt you.

adv_u32.exp:
Unit 32 (Advanced) — estilo indirecto: panorama completo.

Correlación de tiempos (backshift) con reporting-verb en pasado: Present → Past, Past → Past Perfect, Present Perfect → Past Perfect, will → would, can → could, may → might, must → had to/must (queda). «I am tired,» she said → She said (that) she was tired.

Backshift opcional si la afirmación sigue siendo cierta: He said the Earth is round. También si el reporting está en Present: She says she is tired.

La deixis cambia: this → that, here → there, now → then, today → that day, tomorrow → the next day/the following day, yesterday → the day before, last week → the week before, next month → the following month. Los pronombres según contexto: «I love my job,» → He said he loved his job.

Say vs tell: tell exige destinatario (She told me (that)…); say — sin destinatario o con to + sb: She said to me.

That suele omitirse en habla: She said she was tired.

Registro: en escritura académica y periodística los reporting verbs se eligen con cuidado: claim, allege, argue, suggest, admit, deny, insist, maintain — cada uno lleva matiz valorativo.
adv_u32.exc:
Present → Past, will → would, can → could, must → had to.

Backshift no obligatorio si el hecho sigue siendo cierto: She said the Earth is round.
mistakes: He said he is tired → He said he was tired — cambio temporal.
She told that she left → She said (that) she had left / She told me that… — tell exige objeto.
He asked where do I live → He asked where I lived — orden directo.

adv_u33.note: Preguntas, órdenes, exclamaciones en discurso indirecto.
adv_u33.exp:
Unit 33 (Advanced) — preguntas, órdenes y exclamaciones indirectas.

Wh-questions indirectas: orden directo, sin do/does/did, sin signo de interrogación: «Where do you live?» → She asked (me) where I lived.

Yes/no indirectas: con if o whether: «Are you coming?» → He asked if/whether I was coming. Whether más formal; obligado con or not: I don't know whether or not to go.

Órdenes y peticiones: tell/ask/order/warn/advise/beg/urge + sb + (not) to V: «Don't be late!» → She told me not to be late; «Please help me,» → He asked me to help.

Propuestas y consejos: suggest + -ing / suggest that + clause; recommend + -ing / + that; propose + -ing / + that. She suggested going for a walk / She suggested (that) we go for a walk. Subjunctive that + base V se conserva en AmE formal: He insisted that she be there.

Exclamaciones: exclaim, cry, shout + that: «What a mess!» → She exclaimed that it was a mess.

Pragmática: la elección del reporting-verb transmite el tono original: snap, mutter, whisper, protest, concede — no solo «dijo», sino con caracterización del modo.
adv_u33.exc:
Preguntas en reported: sin inversión y sin do/does/did: She asked if I was ready.

Órdenes: tell/ask sb to do.
mistakes: She asked me do I want tea → She asked if I wanted tea.
He told me don't go → He told me not to go.
She said me to sit → She told me to sit.

adv_u34.note: Me pregunto qué quiso decir. La pregunta es quién...
adv_u34.exp:
Unit 34 (Advanced) — wh-subordinadas (wh-clauses) como constituyentes nominales.

La wh-clause (what, who, where, when, why, how, whether, which, whose + clause) funciona como sustantivo y ocupa su posición.

Como objeto: I don't know what he wants; Tell me where you've been; She wondered how he had managed it.

Como sujeto: What she said surprised everyone; How they found us remains a mystery. Típico en registro formal.

Tras preposiciones: It depends on what you want; We argued about why it happened.

Tras sustantivos (tras question, issue, fact, reason, idea): The question of who should lead is unresolved; I have no idea why she left.

Con it-extraposition: It is unclear whether she'll come; It's amazing how fast he learns. Frecuente en texto académico para llevar la cláusula larga al final.

Regla clave — orden directo dentro de la cláusula: I don't know where he lives (NO *where does he live). El fallo delata al estudiante.

Whether vs if: whether universal (sujeto, tras preposición, ante to V); if — solo como objeto: I don't know if/whether he'll come, pero solo Whether he comes is uncertain.
adv_u34.exc: En wh-clauses el orden es afirmativo: I wonder what she meant (no what did she mean).
mistakes: I don't know where is he → I don't know where he is.
Tell me what did you do → Tell me what you did.
The question is who will come? (embedded) → The question is who will come.

adv_u35.text: Unidad 35 – Informes y alternancia de tiempos verbales
adv_u35.note: Cuándo cambiar el tiempo y cuándo no
adv_u35.exp:
Unit 35 (Advanced) — elección de tiempos en estilo indirecto: cuándo desplazar, cuándo no.

Backshift obligatorio cuando el reporting-verb está en pasado y el contenido se vincula a ese momento o puede ya no ser cierto: «I'm busy,» she said → She said she was busy (quizá ya no lo esté).

Backshift opcional si:
  • La información sigue siendo actual: She said she is/was pregnant (sigue embarazada).
  • Es una verdad general/hecho permanente: He said water boils at 100°C.
  • Reporting verb en Present/Present Perfect/Future: She says/has said/will say she is tired.
  • Pequeña distancia entre el evento y su relato.

Backshift imposible con Past Perfect (no hay más hacia atrás) y con los modales would, could, might, should, ought to, had better, used to — se quedan.

Matiz semántico sutil: He said he works in London (señalo: sigue así) vs He said he worked in London (neutral, sin comentario actual). La forma refleja la posición del narrador sobre la veracidad.

Registro: en noticias y textos académicos predomina el backshift estricto; en habla los hablantes usan libremente el presente para información fresca y viva.

adv_u36.note: Ella sugirió ir / Ella propuso que fuéramos
adv_u36.exp:
Unit 36 — reporte de sugerencias, órdenes e intenciones.

Fenómeno: transmisión de la voluntad y planes de una tercera persona mediante verbos específicos con diferentes complementos.

Estructuras:
  • suggest/recommend/propose + -ing o + that-clause (a menudo con subjuntivo/should): She suggested going / She suggested (that) we go. — Ella sugirió ir.
  • order/tell/urge/advise/warn/beg + obj + to-inf: He urged us to reconsider. — Él nos instó a reconsiderar.
  • say/claim + to-inf para intenciones: She said she intended to resign. — Dijo que tenía intención de dimitir.
Registro: la cláusula con that es más formal que el gerundio. Recommend, advise admiten ambas variantes. Contraste: suggest NUNCA admite obj + to-inf (a diferencia de tell, advise). En C1 es importante distinguir entre advise sb to do (indicación directa a una persona) y advise doing (recomendación general).
adv_u36.exc: Suggest no admite la construcción to + inf con objeto: suggest me to go → suggest (that) I go / suggest going.
mistakes: She suggested me to leave. → She suggested (that) I leave / suggested leaving. — suggest + ing o that-clause.
He proposed me a plan. → He proposed a plan to me. — propose no admite doble objeto.
They ordered that he leaves. → They ordered that he leave / to leave. — tras order se usa subjuntivo o infinitivo.

adv_u37.exp:
Unit 37 — verbos modales en estilo indirecto.

Fenómeno: el backshift de los modales al pasar al reported speech no es simétrico: una parte cambia y otra permanece igual.

Cambios: will→would, can→could, may→might, shall→should, must (obligación)→had to. No cambian: would, could, should, might, ought to, used to, had better.

Ejemplos:
  • "I can help." → She said she could help. — Ella dijo que podía ayudar.
  • "You must leave." → He said I had to leave. — Él dijo que yo tenía que irme.
  • "It must be true." → He said it must be true. — Dijo que debía de ser verdad (el must epistémico se conserva).
Contraste: must con valor de deducción lógica normalmente no cambia; con valor de obligación pasa a had to. Cuando el discurso y el hecho son simultáneos, el cambio puede omitirse: She says she will come.
adv_u37.exc: Must de obligación suele pasar a had to; el de deducción lógica se mantiene: He said it must be true.
mistakes: He said he must go yesterday. → He said he had to go. — must de obligación pasa a had to.
She said she can swim. → She said she could swim. — cambio can → could en reported speech.
He told that he would come. → He said (that) he would come. — tell requiere objeto.

adv_u38.text: Unidad 38 – Informar con sustantivos y adjetivos
adv_u38.note: Su afirmación de que… / Su negativa a… / el hecho de que…
adv_u38.exp:
Unit 38 — reporte mediante sustantivos y adjetivos.

Fenómeno: las palabras y posturas ajenas se transmiten con construcciones nominales y adjetivales compactas, típicas del estilo académico y periodístico.

Estructuras:
  • Sustantivo + cláusula con that: claim, belief, suggestion, admission, denial, acknowledgement, announcement: The claim that he lied was unfounded. — La afirmación de que mintió resultó infundada.
  • Sustantivo + to-inf: refusal, decision, willingness, reluctance, intention: Her refusal to cooperate surprised us. — Su negativa a cooperar nos sorprendió.
  • Adjetivo + cláusula con that/to-inf: adamant that, reluctant to, insistent that: He was adamant that he was right. — Insistía tercamente en que tenía razón.
Registro: la nominalización eleva la formalidad. Contraste: the fact that sin of; tras advice/news solo cláusula con that, no infinitivo.
adv_u38.exc: Tras the fact that no se usa of: the fact of that → the fact that. Tras news / advice va cláusula con that, no to-inf.
mistakes: His refusal of help us → His refusal to help us — refusal + to-inf.
The claim of he was innocent → The claim that he was innocent — claim + cláusula con that.
An advice that he gave → The advice (that) he gave — advice es incontable.

adv_u39.text: Unidad 39 – Debería en esas cláusulas; presente de subjuntivo
adv_u39.note: Es importante que venga / que venga
adv_u39.exp:
Unit 39 — should en cláusulas con that y presente subjuntivo.

Fenómeno: tras verbos y expresiones de exigencia o importancia se usa una forma especial: o should + inf (BrE), o la forma base del verbo sin -s (subjuntivo, AmE y formal).

Detonantes: verbos suggest, recommend, insist, demand, require, propose, request, urge; adjetivos essential, important, vital, crucial, necessary, imperative; sustantivos demand, recommendation, insistence.

Ejemplos:
  • I insist that he be present. (AmE) / …that he should be present. (BrE) — Insisto en que esté presente.
  • It is essential that she not miss the meeting. — Es esencial que no falte a la reunión (nota: not va antes del verbo).
Contraste: insist that + indicativo = constatación de un hecho (He insisted that he was right); + subjuntivo/should = exigencia (He insisted that she leave). Registro del subjuntivo: escrito y académico.
adv_u39.exc: En BrE es habitual should, en AmE la forma base (subjuntivo): I suggest (that) he be present.
mistakes: It is essential that he comes. → It is essential that he come / should come. — subjuntivo tras essential.
I demand that she is here. → I demand that she be here. — forma base tras demand.
He insisted that she went. → He insisted that she go / should go. — tras insist con valor de exigencia.

adv_u40.text: Unidad 40 – Concordancia entre sujeto y verbo (1)
adv_u40.note: Problemas de concordancia de sujeto y predicado.
adv_u40.exp:
Unit 40 — concordancia entre sujeto y verbo (parte 1).

Fenómeno: elección del número del verbo cuando el sujeto es complejo o formalmente ambiguo.

Reglas:
  • Colectivos (team, government, family, committee): BrE sing. o pl. según el sentido (the team are arguing = individuos), AmE solo sing.
  • Neither…nor / Either…or: concordancia con el sujeto más cercano: Neither the students nor the teacher was aware.
  • Sujeto = infinitivo/cláusula: sing.: To err is human. What matters is effort.
  • Distancias, sumas y tiempos como unidad: sing.: Ten miles is a long walk. £500 is enough.
Excepciones: news, mathematics, physics, politics, economics son formalmente plurales pero llevan verbo en sing.; police, cattle, people siempre en plural. Contraste: a pair of trousers is / trousers are, según la estructura.
adv_u40.exc:
The news / mathematics / politics son formalmente plurales pero llevan verbo en sing.
Police / cattle siempre en plural.
mistakes: The news are bad. → The news is bad. — news en singular.
Police is coming. → Police are coming. — police en plural.
Everyone are here. → Everyone is here. — everyone en singular.

adv_u41.text: Unidad 41 – Concordancia entre sujeto y verbo (2)
adv_u41.exp:
Unit 41 — concordancia entre sujeto y verbo (parte 2).

Fenómeno: continuación del tema: expresiones de cantidad y pronombres indefinidos.

Reglas:
  • A number of + sust. pl. = verbo en plural: A number of students were absent. — Varios alumnos faltaron.
  • The number of + sust. pl. = verbo en sing.: The number of applicants is rising. — El número de solicitantes está aumentando.
  • Fracciones y porcentajes: concordancia con el sust. de referencia: Half of the cake is gone / Half of the students are here.
  • Indefinidos everyone, someone, nobody, each, either, neither: sing.: Everyone is ready.
Registro: formalmente neither of them knows; coloquialmente también know. Contraste: a lot of y plenty of: concordancia con el sust. (a lot of water is / a lot of people are).
adv_u41.exc: A number of + verbo en plural; the number of + verbo en singular.
mistakes: A number of students was absent. → A number of students were absent. — a number of + plural.
The number of errors are rising. → The number of errors is rising. — the number = singular.
Neither of them know. → Neither of them knows. — neither formal sing.

adv_u42.text: Unidad 42 – Concordancia entre sujeto y verbo (3)
adv_u42.note: Acuerdo con cláusulas relativas y temas complejos
adv_u42.exp:
Unit 42 — concordancia entre sujeto y verbo (parte 3).

Fenómeno: concordancia en oraciones de relativo y con sujetos numéricos o de grupo complejos.

Reglas:
  • En one of the X who/that + V: el verbo va en plural porque el antecedente es X (pl.): She is one of those people who care deeply. — Es de esas personas que se preocupan profundamente.
  • En the only one of the X who/that: verbo en sing.: He's the only one of us who knows.
  • Numerales como suma/medida: sing.: Three million pounds was raised. — Se recaudaron tres millones de libras.
  • The majority/minority of: concordancia con el sust.: The majority of voters support…
Contraste: more than one + verbo en sing. (contra el sentido): More than one student has complained. El estilo académico formal exige una elección clara del referente.
adv_u42.exc: En one of the X who/that + V el verbo va en plural: He is one of the people who work here.
mistakes: He's one of those who likes tea. → …who like tea. — antecedente en plural.
Either you or I is wrong. → Either you or I am wrong. — concordancia con el sujeto más cercano.
The team are winning the cup (AmE). → The team is winning. — en AmE los colectivos son sing.

adv_u43.text: Unidad 43 – Sustantivos compuestos y frases nominales
adv_u43.note: Aparcamiento / dos horas de retraso / última generación
adv_u43.exp:
Unit 43 — sustantivos compuestos y sintagmas nominales.

Fenómeno: formaciones nominales complejas en textos formales. Alternativa compacta a las construcciones preposicionales y de modificación.

Estructuras:
  • Compuestos: car park, science fiction, bookshop; la ortografía (junto/con guion/separado) no sigue una regla general.
  • Posición atributiva: primer sustantivo en sing., a menudo con guion: a ten-minute walk, a five-year-old child, a two-hour meeting.
  • Posmodificación con of: a walk of ten minutes (menos compacto).
Ejemplos:
  • A state-of-the-art facility. — Una instalación de última generación.
  • Student accommodation problems. — Problemas de alojamiento estudiantil (cadena de tres sust.).
Registro: las cadenas largas son típicas de la prensa y del estilo académico. Contraste: a woman doctor (tipo) vs a doctor's office (pertenencia): relaciones distintas.
adv_u43.exc: En los sustantivos compuestos el primer elemento suele ir en sing.: a two-hour delay, no a two-hours delay.
mistakes: A five-years-old boy → A five-year-old boy — sin -s en posición atributiva.
A cars park → A car park — primer sust. en sing.
An information centre (si se piensa en «centro de informaciones») → An information centre — information es incontable.

adv_u44.note: un amigo mío / una persona que conozco
adv_u44.exp:
Unit 44 — a/an y one.

Fenómeno: ambas palabras pueden indicar «uno», pero tienen funciones distintas: artículo vs. numeral/pronombre de énfasis.

Diferencias:
  • A/an: artículo indefinido neutro: I need a pen. — Necesito un bolígrafo.
  • One: énfasis en el número o contraste: I need one pen, not two. — Necesito un bolígrafo, no dos.
  • One + sust. = «cierto/determinado»: One day you will understand. — Algún día lo entenderás.
  • A/an obligatorio con profesiones y unidades de medida: She's a teacher. £50 an hour.
Expresiones con one: one day, one morning, one of these days, one and the same. Contraste: Wait a minute (neutro) vs Wait one minute! (énfasis, enfado). En expresiones de tiempo/precio solo a/an: twice a week, no twice one week.
adv_u44.exc: One subraya «exactamente uno»; a/an es neutro: Wait a minute vs Wait one minute (énfasis).
mistakes: I have one brother and two sister. → …two sisters. — plural tras un número mayor que 1.
She is a my friend. → She is a friend of mine. — no se admite un doble determinante.
One day or another → One day or other / some day. — expresión fija.

adv_u45.text: Unidad 45 - A/an, el artículo y cero (1)
adv_u45.note: Principios básicos para elegir un artículo en C1
adv_u45.exp:
Unit 45 — a/an, the y artículo cero (parte 1).

Fenómeno: sistematización del uso del artículo según las categorías de definición, especificidad y generalización.

Tipos de referencia:
  • Específica vs. general: The tiger is endangered (la especie en su conjunto) / A tiger is dangerous (cualquier representante del tipo) / Tigers are endangered (generalización en plural, la más neutra).
  • Progresión temática: a introduce lo nuevo, the lo retoma: I saw a man. The man was tall.
  • Objetos únicos: the sun, the moon, the president (of…).
Ejemplos:
  • The computer has changed society. — El ordenador ha cambiado la sociedad (como invento).
  • She plays the violin. — Toca el violín (instrumento con the).
Contraste: en el estilo científico se prefiere el plural sin artículo (Tigers are…) frente a the tiger. Topónimos: the Alps, the Netherlands, the UK: excepciones que hay que memorizar.
adv_u45.exc: Con nombres propios normalmente no hay artículo, pero: the Netherlands, the UK, the Alps.
mistakes: I love the nature. → I love nature. — los conceptos abstractos van sin the.
She plays the football. → She plays football. — los deportes sin artículo.
He goes to the bed early. → He goes to bed early. — expresión fija sin artículo.

adv_u46.text: Unidad 46 - A/an, el artículo y cero (2)
adv_u46.exp:
Unit 46 — a/an, the y artículo cero (parte 2).

Fenómeno: uso del artículo con sustantivos abstractos e incontables; matices de significado al cambiar de artículo.

Reglas:
  • Concepto general: artículo cero: Life is short. Love conquers all.
  • Concepto concreto: the: The life of a monk is hard. The love of money is the root of all evil.
  • Tipo o manifestación: a/an: She has a deep love of music. — Tiene una profunda pasión por la música.
Expresiones fijas sin artículo: at war, in love, by chance, on fire, at risk, in hospital, at school, by car.

Ejemplos:
  • Happiness is hard to define. — La felicidad es difícil de definir.
  • He felt a happiness he had never known. — Sintió una felicidad que no había conocido antes.
Contraste: Man is mortal (generalización formal) vs Humans are mortal (neutro).
adv_u46.exc: Man/woman sin artículo para referirse a la humanidad: Man is mortal (formal).
mistakes: The life is hard. → Life is hard. — generalización sin artículo.
Dogs are the man's best friend. → Dogs are man's best friend. — expresión fija.
The happiness is important. → Happiness is important. — abstracto sin artículo.

adv_u47.text: Unidad 47 - A/an, el artículo y cero (3)
adv_u47.exp:
Unit 47 — artículos en grupos temáticos.

Fenómeno: uso fijo del artículo con instituciones, transportes, enfermedades, comidas e instrumentos musicales.

Reglas:
  • Institución por su función: sin artículo: in hospital (como paciente), at school (como alumno), in prison (como preso), in church (en misa).
  • El mismo lugar como objeto físico: con the: at the hospital (ir de visita), near the school.
  • Transporte con by: cero: by train, by bus, by plane, pero on the train, in the car.
  • Enfermedades: normalmente cero (cancer, diabetes); infecciones/malestares: a (a cold, a headache); en AmE: the flu, the measles.
  • Instrumentos: play the piano; deportes: play football.
Contraste: BrE go to hospital vs AmE go to the hospital. Go to bed (dormir) vs sit on the bed (en la cama como mueble).
adv_u47.exc: Go to hospital (BrE, como paciente) vs go to the hospital (AmE / como visitante).
mistakes: He's in the prison. → He's in prison. — como preso.
I go to the work by bus. → I go to work by bus. — by + transporte sin artículo.
She has flu. (AmE) → She has the flu. — en AmE con the.

adv_u48.exp:
Unit 48 — some y any en C1.

Fenómeno: la elección depende no solo del tipo de oración, sino también del significado (referencial vs. no referencial).

Reglas:
  • Some: cantidad u objeto conocido pero no concretado: I read it in some book. — Lo leí en algún libro.
  • Any en afirmativas = «cualquier, sin excepción»: Any student can apply. — Cualquier alumno puede solicitarlo.
  • Some en preguntas: cuando se espera aceptación o en ofrecimientos corteses: Would you like some tea?
  • Any en preguntas y negaciones: neutro: Is there any milk?
Expresiones: some day, somehow, somewhere (indeterminación en el futuro); anyhow, anyway (en cualquier caso).

Contraste: Pick any card (cualquiera) vs Pick some cards (algunas). If you have any questions (cualesquiera) vs If you have some questions (algunas concretas ya surgidas).
adv_u48.exc: Any en afirmativas = «cualquier»: Pick any card. Some en preguntas cuando se espera «sí»: Would you like some tea?
mistakes: I don't have some money. → I don't have any money. — any en negaciones.
Is there some milk? (pregunta neutra) → Is there any milk?
Anybody can't do it. → Nobody can do it / Anybody can do it. — doble negación.

adv_u49.text: Unidad 49 – No, ninguno (de) y no ninguno
adv_u49.note: Ninguna solución / ninguna de ellas / ningún motivo
adv_u49.exp:
Unit 49 — no, none (of) y not any.

Fenómeno: tres maneras de expresar ausencia, con registros y sintaxis diferentes.

Diferencias:
  • No + sust.: rotundo, enfático, habitual en titulares y textos formales: There are no seats left. — No quedan asientos.
  • Not any: equivalente neutro y coloquial: There aren't any seats left.
  • None: pronombre independiente, sin sustantivo: How many came? — None.
  • None of + sintagma definido: None of the students passed.
Concordancia: none of + pl.: formalmente sing. (None of them was ready), coloquialmente pl. (None of them were ready).

Contraste: No smoking (prohibición) ≠ Not smoking (ausencia de la acción). No se pueden combinar: I have no any money es doble negación errónea.
adv_u49.exc: Tras none of + plural, formalmente sing., pero se admite plural: None of us is/are ready.
mistakes: I have no any money. → I have no money / don't have any money. — no se combinan.
None of them was invited (coloq.) → None of them were invited. — el plural es más frecuente en la lengua coloquial.
No of them came. → None of them came. — antes de of se usa none.

adv_u50.exp:
Unit 50 — much (of), many (of), a lot of, lots (of).

Fenómeno: expresiones de cantidad con grandes diferencias de registro.

Escala de registro:
  • A great deal of / a large amount of / much: formal (estilo escrito): A great deal of effort was required.
  • A lot of / plenty of: neutro, universal.
  • Lots of / loads of / tons of: coloquial: We had loads of fun.
Reglas: much en afirmativas se evita: se sustituye por a lot of. En negaciones y preguntas much/many son neutros: Do you have much time? Not much. Many of + def.: Many of the problems remain unsolved.

Concordancia: a lot of water is / a lot of people are — con el sust.

Contraste: much sí se admite en afirmativas con modificadores: so much time, too much noise, as much as possible.
adv_u50.exc: Much es raro en afirmativas: evita I have much money → I have a lot of money.
mistakes: I have much friends. → I have many / a lot of friends. — contable.
A lot of water are needed. → A lot of water is needed. — concordancia con water.
Lots of informations → Lots of information. — incontable.

adv_u51.text: Unidad 51 – Todo (de), todo, cada, cada uno
adv_u51.note: Diferencias sutiles, contextos formales y coloquiales.
adv_u51.exp:
Unit 51 — all (of), whole, every, each.

Fenómeno: cuatro cuantificadores «totalizadores» con lógicas distintas.

Diferencias:
  • All: la totalidad, de forma colectiva: All the students passed.
  • Whole: integridad, sin excepción, con énfasis en la unidad: The whole class passed. / the whole day.
  • Every: distributivo, cada uno sin excepción (3+ elementos): Every student got a certificate.
  • Each: individualiza, a menudo 2+ elementos con atención a las diferencias: Each student received a different task.
Posposición: They all arrived / They each received a copy (tras el pronombre).

Contraste: all day = the whole day (24 horas), pero every day = cada día de una serie. Each of them knows (siempre en sing.). Every no admite of: hay que decir every one of.
adv_u51.exc: All / whole: all the day (raro) vs the whole day. Con expresiones de tiempo suele usarse the whole.
mistakes: Every students → Every student — sing.
Each of them know. → Each of them knows. — each en sing.
All of information → All the information / all of the information.

adv_u52.note: Menos errores versus menos dinero (y con menos errores)
adv_u52.exp:
Unit 52 — few, little, less, fewer.

Fenómeno: oposición de cantidad según el tipo de sustantivo y la connotación.

Reglas:
  • Few: con contables pl.: few friends (pocos, negativo). A few: positivo (algunos): a few friends.
  • Little: con incontables: little time (poco). A little: positivo: a little time.
  • Fewer: comparativo de few: fewer cars.
  • Less: comparativo de little: less traffic.
Ejemplos:
  • He has few real friends. — Tiene pocos amigos de verdad.
  • She has a little patience left. — Le queda un poco de paciencia.
Uso coloquial: less people en lugar de fewer people está muy extendido, pero en la norma escrita es un error. Equivalentes formales de few/little: scarcely any, hardly any. Contraste: quite a few (bastantes, positivo) vs few (pocos).
adv_u52.exc: Less con incontables; fewer con contables. Coloquialmente aparece less cars, pero no en la norma escrita.
mistakes: Less people came. → Fewer people came. — contable.
A few water → A little water. — incontable.
Little friends → Few friends — contable.

adv_u53.exp:
Unit 53 — pronombres relativos.

Fenómeno: distinción entre oraciones relativas especificativas (defining) y explicativas (non-defining) y elección del pronombre.

Reglas:
  • Especificativas (sin comas, información necesaria): who/that/which/whom/whose. Whom como objeto suele omitirse en el habla.
  • Explicativas (con comas, información añadida): solo who/whom/which/whose; that es IMPOSIBLE.
  • Objeto who/that/which puede omitirse en especificativas: The book (which) I read.
  • Which para referirse a toda la oración (solo explicativas): She was late, which annoyed me.
Registro: whom es formal; who se usa como objeto en el habla.

Ejemplos:
  • My sister, who lives in Paris, is a doctor. (tengo una sola hermana, aclaración)
  • The sister who lives in Paris is a doctor. (una de varias hermanas, identifica)
Contraste: las comas cambian el sentido del enunciado.
adv_u53.exc: En las oraciones explicativas no se usa that: My sister, that lives in NY → My sister, who lives in NY.
mistakes: The book what I read → The book (that/which) I read. — what no se usa como relativo.
The man which helped me → The man who helped me. — para personas.
My brother, that is a doctor → My brother, who is a doctor.

adv_u54.text: Unidad 54 – De quién, cuándo, por qué y otras palabras relativas
adv_u54.note: El sistema por el cual… / la razón por la cual…
adv_u54.exp:
Unit 54 — whose, when, whereby y otros relativos.

Fenómeno: conjunto ampliado de relativos propios del estilo académico y jurídico.

Palabras:
  • Whose: pertenencia (personas y cosas): A country whose economy is growing.
  • Whereby = by which: A system whereby students apply online.
  • Wherein = in which (muy formal).
  • Whereupon: «y tras eso»: He nodded, whereupon the meeting ended.
  • When: tiempo: The day when we met.
  • Where: lugar: The town where I grew up.
  • Why: causa: The reason why he left.
Registro: whereby/wherein/whereupon: solo estilo formal escrito.

Contraste: where ya incluye la preposición: no se puede the place where I was born in. The reason why es coloquial; formalmente, the reason for which.
adv_u54.exc: Whose puede referirse también a cosas: a country whose population is growing.
mistakes: The reason because → The reason why / that / for which.
The place where I was born in → The place where I was born. — where ya incluye «en».
The day when we met on → The day (when) we met.

adv_u55.note: La empresa para la cual / la persona a quien
adv_u55.exp:
Unit 55 — preposiciones en oraciones de relativo.

Fenómeno: la posición de la preposición es un marcador clave de registro entre el estilo formal y el coloquial.

Variantes:
  • Coloquial: preposición al final, who/that u omisión: The person (who/that) I spoke to.
  • Formal: preposición antes de whom/which: The person to whom I spoke.
Restricciones: tras preposición no se usa who ni that: solo whom/which. No puede omitirse el pronombre si la preposición va delante.

Ejemplos:
  • The company for which I work is expanding. (formal) — La empresa en la que trabajo se está expandiendo.
  • The topic we were talking about was interesting. (coloq.)
Registro: los verbos con preposición exigen conservarla (look after → the child I look after). Contraste: la escritura académica prefiere of which/in which/to whom; el correo y el habla, la preposición al final.
adv_u55.exc: Formal: the man to whom I spoke; informal: the man (who) I spoke to.
mistakes: The company which I work → The company which I work for / for which I work.
The person to who I wrote → The person to whom I wrote. — tras to va whom.
The issue about that we talked → The issue (which/that) we talked about.

adv_u56.text: Unidad 56 – Frases sustantivas en una oración (Lección 1)
adv_u56.exp:
Unit 56 — sintagmas nominales (lección 1).

Fenómeno: las noun phrases son una herramienta clave para empaquetar información de forma densa en textos formales.

Estructura: determiner + pre-modifier(s) + head noun + post-modifier(s).
  • Premodificadores: adjetivos (beautiful), sustantivos (car park), participios (running water), cadenas atributivas.
  • Posmodificadores: oración de relativo (…that I read), sintagma preposicional (…on the table), infinitivo (the first to arrive), construcción de participio (people living abroad).
Orden de los adjetivos: opinión → tamaño → edad → forma → color → origen → material → propósito: a lovely little old round red Italian wooden dining table.

Ejemplo: The recently published book on climate change by the leading scientist. — El libro sobre cambio climático publicado recientemente por el científico más destacado.

Contraste: el estilo académico prefiere la nominalización (the publication of the book) a las oraciones verbales (when they published the book).
adv_u56.exc: En el lenguaje formal las noun phrases pueden ser muy largas gracias a la posmodificación.
mistakes: The book interesting → The interesting book. — un adjetivo simple va delante del sust.
A man tall and dark → A tall, dark man.
The girl her hair is long → The girl whose hair is long.

adv_u57.exp:
Unit 57 — sintagmas nominales (lección 2): posmodificación.

Fenómeno: formas reducidas de oraciones de relativo: un recurso académico económico.

Tipos de posmodificadores:
  • De participio: the woman standing there = who is standing there; the car parked outside = which is parked outside.
  • De infinitivo: the first person to arrive; the only one to notice.
  • Sintagmas preposicionales: the man in the blue shirt.
  • Aposición: my friend Anna, a talented pianist.
Ejemplos:
  • The issues discussed at the meeting remain unresolved. — Los temas tratados en la reunión siguen sin resolver.
  • Letters to be posted should be left here. — Las cartas que hay que enviar deben dejarse aquí.
Contraste: the man running (acción activa ahora) vs the man who runs (habitual). El posmodificador to be-infinitive aporta matiz de necesidad o futuro.
adv_u57.exc: Posmodificadores de participio y de infinitivo: the man standing there / the first to arrive.
mistakes: The man who is sitting there → The man sitting there. — reducción.
The problems which are discussed → The problems discussed.
Letters to posting → Letters to post / to be posted.
